natural_occurrence.description: While sulfuric acid can form naturally through oxidation of sulfur dioxide in volcanic emissions and acid rain, all commercial sulfuric acid is synthetically produced.

manufacturing.description: Produced primarily via the contact process: sulfur is burned to produce sulfur dioxide (SO2), which is then oxidized to sulfur trioxide (SO3) using a vanadium pentoxide catalyst, and finally absorbed in water to form sulfuric acid. Food-grade sulfuric acid must be of high purity.

other_uses.cosmetics: Rarely used; pH adjuster in some chemical peels (professional use only).
other_uses.industrial: Fertilizer production (largest use), petroleum refining, metal processing, battery acid, chemical manufacturing.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Manufacturing of various pharmaceuticals, pH adjustment in drug formulations.
other_uses.household: Drain cleaners (industrial grade, not food grade), car batteries.

eu.conditions: Used strictly as processing aid and pH regulator. Must be neutralized in final product.

usa.notes: Used primarily in food processing rather than as direct ingredient.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no residue in finished food)

commonly_found_in: Sugar refining (processing aid - not in final product)
fun_facts: One of the most important industrial chemicals in the world - production volume is an economic indicator
Despite its fearsome reputation as 'battery acid', food-grade sulfuric acid is neutralized during processing
When used in food manufacturing, it's always converted to sulfate salts - no free acid remains in the final product
Historically known as 'oil of vitriol' because it was originally made from green vitriol (iron sulfate)
The same acid that causes acid rain when sulfur dioxide pollution mixes with atmospheric moisture
Used in sugar refining to break down impurities, then neutralized with lime to form harmless calcium sulfate (gypsum)
